walkthrough.title: Meet the Board
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Backgammon, one of the oldest board games in the world. Let's take a look at the board. You will see 24 long, narrow triangles called points, alternating in two colors. The points are arranged in four groups of six, forming four quadrants. A raised bar runs down the center of the board, dividing it into two halves.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Quadrants
walkthrough.text: Each player has a home board and an outer board. Your home board is the quadrant where you will bear off your checkers at the end of the game, containing points one through six. Your outer board is the quadrant next to it, containing points seven through twelve. Your opponent's home and outer boards mirror yours on the other side of the bar.

walkthrough.title: Get Your Checkers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 15 checkers in their color. One player takes all the light checkers and the other takes all the dark checkers. You will also need a pair of standard six-sided dice for each player. Set the dice aside for now while we place the checkers.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Checkers — The 24-Point
walkthrough.text: Let's set up the starting position. Place two of your checkers on your 24-point. That is the point farthest from your home board, deep in your opponent's home board. These two checkers have the longest journey ahead of them.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Checkers — The 13-Point
walkthrough.text: This point sits in your opponent's outer board, just past the halfway mark. These five checkers form your largest group at the start and serve as a strong anchor on your opponent's side of the board.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Checkers — The 8-Point
walkthrough.text: This point is in your own outer board, just two points away from your home board. These three checkers are close to home and will be among the first you bring in for bearing off.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Checkers — The 6-Point
walkthrough.text: This is the first point of your home board. You now have all 15 checkers on the board in the classic starting arrangement: two on the 24-point, five on the 13-point, three on the 8-point, and five on the 6-point. Your opponent mirrors this setup exactly from their perspective.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Doubling Cube
walkthrough.text: Find the doubling cube. It is a large die with the numbers 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, and 64 on its faces. Place it in the center of the bar with the 64 face up, indicating that no one has doubled yet. We will explain how it works later, but for now just set it in the middle where both players can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Determine Who Goes First
walkthrough.text: Each player rolls one die. The player who rolls the higher number goes first, using the two numbers just rolled as their opening move. If both players roll the same number, roll again until someone wins. Let's learn how to move.

4 quadrants of 6 points each, divided by a center bar

Opponent's boards mirror yours across the bar

bullets: 3 checkers on the 8-point (your outer board)

bullets: 5 checkers on the 6-point (your home board)
Total: 2 + 5 + 3 + 5 = 15 checkers
Opponent mirrors the same layout

summary.title: Set Up the Doubling Cube
bullets: Large die showing 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64
Place centered on the bar with 64 face up

summary.title: Determine Who Goes First

First move uses both numbers rolled
Tie → re-roll

walkthrough.title: Direction of Movement
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about how checkers move. Both players move their checkers in opposite directions around the board. You move from higher-numbered points toward lower-numbered points, ultimately into your home board. Your opponent does the same from their perspective, so your checkers travel in a horseshoe pattern past each other.

walkthrough.title: Rolling and Moving
walkthrough.text: On your turn, roll both dice. Each die is a separate move. If you roll a three and a five, you must move one checker exactly three points and one checker exactly five points. You may move the same checker twice, first three then five, or move two different checkers. The key is that each die produces one distinct move of exactly that many points.

walkthrough.title: Doubles
walkthrough.text: When you roll doubles, you get four moves instead of two. For example, if you roll double fours, you have four separate moves of four points each. You may split these across up to four different checkers or use them all on one checker. Doubles are powerful turns that can swing the game.

walkthrough.title: Where You Can Land
walkthrough.text: A checker may land on any point that is open. A point is open if it has no checkers on it, has your own checkers on it, or has exactly one of your opponent's checkers on it. You cannot land on a point that has two or more of your opponent's checkers. There is no limit to how many of your own checkers can stack on a single point.

walkthrough.title: Hitting a Blot
walkthrough.text: A single checker sitting alone on a point is called a blot. If you land on your opponent's blot, you hit it. The hit checker is removed from the point and placed on the bar in the center of the board. This is one of the most important tactical moves in backgammon, because the hit checker must start its journey over from the very beginning.

walkthrough.title: Re-entering from the Bar
walkthrough.text: If you have any checkers on the bar, you must re-enter them before making any other moves. To re-enter, roll your dice and place the checker on the corresponding point in your opponent's home board. A roll of three means you enter on the opponent's three-point. The point must be open, just like any normal move. If neither die lets you enter, your entire turn is lost.

walkthrough.title: Using Both Dice
walkthrough.text: You must use both dice if it is legally possible. If you can only play one die, you must play the higher value. If neither die can be used, your turn is skipped. You cannot voluntarily forfeit a legal move even if playing it puts you in a worse position.

walkthrough.title: Building a Prime
walkthrough.text: When you stack two or more checkers on a point, that point is closed to your opponent. If you close six consecutive points in a row, you have built a prime. An opponent's checker behind a prime is completely trapped and cannot pass, because no single die roll goes higher than six. Building primes is one of the most powerful strategies in the game.

walkthrough.title: Bearing Off
walkthrough.text: Once all 15 of your checkers are in your home board, you begin bearing off. To bear off, roll your dice and remove a checker from the point matching each die. If you roll a four, you may remove a checker from your four-point. If no checker is on the matching point, you must move a checker from a higher point instead. If no checkers are on higher points, you may remove a checker from the highest occupied point.

walkthrough.title: Getting Hit While Bearing Off
walkthrough.text: If one of your checkers gets hit while you are bearing off, you must bring it back into play and move it all the way around the board into your home board again before you can continue bearing off. This is devastating, which is why protecting your blots during bearing off is critical.

walkthrough.title: The Doubling Cube
walkthrough.text: Before rolling on your turn, you may propose to double the stakes by offering the doubling cube. Your opponent must either accept the double or forfeit the game at the current stake. If they accept, they take the cube and place it on their side with the next higher number showing. Only the player holding the cube may propose the next double.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The first player to bear off all 15 checkers wins. A normal win is worth one point, multiplied by the doubling cube value. If the loser has not borne off any checkers, it is a gammon worth two times the stake. If the loser still has a checker on the bar or in the winner's home board, it is a backgammon worth three times the stake.

walkthrough.title: Welcome — Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Let's play through the opening of a two-player backgammon game together. We will walk through several turns, and along the way you will see three common mistakes that new players make. Each mistake will become a lesson you can carry into every game from here on. The board is set up with the standard starting position: two checkers on the 24-point, five on the 13-point, three on the 8-point, and five on the 6-point.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Both players roll one die. Player one rolls a four and Player two rolls a two, so Player one goes first using a four and a two as their opening roll. Let's see what Player one does with it.

walkthrough.title: Player One: A Solid Opening
walkthrough.text: Player one uses the four to move a checker from the 24-point to the 20-point, and uses the two to move a checker from the 13-point to the 11-point. Both checkers land on open points. This is a reasonable opening, spreading out to claim territory early.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Leaving a Blot in Danger
walkthrough.text: Player two rolls a six and a one. They move one checker from the 24-point to the 18-point with the six, and another checker from the 13-point to the 12-point with the one. The checker on the 18-point is now sitting alone. That single checker on an exposed point is called a blot, and it is dangerously within range of Player one's checkers.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Hitting the Blot
walkthrough.text: Player one rolls a five and a two. They spot the blot on the 18-point and use the two to move a checker from the 20-point to the 18-point, hitting Player two's blot and sending it to the bar. Player two's checker is removed from the board and placed on the bar. Player one uses the five for another move. This is a devastating setback for Player two.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect Your Checkers — Avoid Leaving Blots
walkthrough.text: A blot is any single checker left alone on a point, and it is vulnerable to being hit. Getting sent to the bar means your checker must re-enter from your opponent's home board and travel the entire length of the board again. Whenever possible, move checkers in pairs or land them on points you already occupy. If you must leave a blot, try to leave it far from your opponent's checkers where it is harder to hit.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Re-entering from the Bar
walkthrough.text: Player two has a checker on the bar and must re-enter it before doing anything else. They roll a three and a five. The three-point in Player one's home board is open, so Player two enters the checker there. Now they can use the five for a normal move. Being sent to the bar cost Player two significant progress.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Using Only One Die
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player two rolls a six and a four. They see a good move with the six and move one checker forward. But they look at the four and decide none of the options are appealing, so they try to skip it and end their turn. This is not allowed. You must use both dice if it is legally possible.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Corrects the Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player one stops the game and points out the error. Let's look at the board together. There are two possible moves for the four, both landing on open points. Player two has to pick one of them. They choose the less risky option that does not leave a blot.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: You Must Use Both Dice
walkthrough.text: Both dice must be played if there are legal moves available for each. If you can only use one of the two dice, you must use the one with the higher value. If neither die can be played, your entire turn is skipped. You cannot voluntarily give up a legal move, even if you would prefer not to make it. Always scan the board carefully for every legal option before declaring your turn over.

walkthrough.title: Building Toward Bearing Off
walkthrough.text: Let's fast-forward several turns. Both players have been racing their checkers around the board. Player one has gathered most of their checkers in the home board. Player two has 14 checkers in the home board, but one checker remains on the 8-point in the outer board. Player two is eager to start bearing off.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Trying to Bear Off Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player two rolls a five and a three. They reach for a checker on the five-point to bear it off. But wait. Player two still has one checker on the 8-point, outside the home board. You cannot bear off any checkers until all 15 are inside your home board. Player two must use this roll to bring that straggler home instead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: All Fifteen Checkers Must Be Home First
walkthrough.text: Bearing off only begins once every single one of your 15 checkers is in your home board. Even one checker outside the home board means you cannot remove any pieces from the board. Plan ahead and bring all your checkers home before you start thinking about bearing off. If a checker gets hit and sent to the bar during bearing off, you must stop and bring it all the way back home before resuming.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Gets Home and Bears Off
walkthrough.text: Player two uses the five to move the checker from the 8-point to the 3-point, bringing it into the home board. Now all 15 checkers are home. On the next turn, Player two rolls a six and a two, bearing off a checker from the 6-point and one from the 2-point. The race to the finish is on.

walkthrough.title: The Race to the End
walkthrough.text: Both players now bear off checkers each turn, removing them from the board with matching die rolls. Player one, who built a strong position early by hitting blots and stacking points, finishes first. Player two still has three checkers left when Player one removes their final piece. Player one wins a normal game, worth one point.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through the opening, middle game, and endgame of backgammon. You saw three critical lessons: protect your checkers from being hit, always use both dice when possible, and get all 15 checkers home before bearing off. You are ready to play a full game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are two things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for advice on opening moves, safe play, and when to take risks.

Second, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck.

summary.title: Welcome — Your First Game

3 common mistakes → 3 lessons
Standard starting position: 2/24, 5/13, 3/8, 5/6

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → opening moves, safe play, risk management
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Making Points
walkthrough.text: The safest checker is one that is not alone. Every time you can stack two or more checkers on the same point, that point becomes closed to your opponent. Try to make points in your home board as early as possible, especially the five-point and the four-point. A strong home board makes it harder for your opponent to re-enter from the bar, and it gives you safe landing spots for your own checkers.

walkthrough.title: Learn the Opening Moves
walkthrough.text: Backgammon has well-known best plays for every opening roll. For example, a roll of three-one should be used to make your five-point, and six-one should make your bar point. Learning even a few standard openings gives you a significant advantage over players who move randomly. You do not need to memorize them all at once, but knowing the most common ones will immediately improve your game.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Blots in Dangerous Zones
walkthrough.text: When you must leave a blot, think about where it is and how easily your opponent can hit it. A blot six points or fewer from an opponent's checker is in direct hitting range from a single die. A blot seven to twelve points away can be hit with a combination of two dice. If you have to leave a blot, try to leave it far from enemy checkers or in a position where getting hit is less damaging.

walkthrough.title: Use the Bar Wisely When You Hit
walkthrough.text: Hitting your opponent's blots is not always the right move. Before hitting, consider whether it will leave your own checker exposed. A hit that leaves you with a blot on a point surrounded by your opponent's checkers can backfire badly. The best hits send your opponent backward while keeping your own checkers safe.

walkthrough.title: Keep an Anchor in Your Opponent's Board
walkthrough.text: An anchor is a point in your opponent's home board where you have two or more checkers. Anchors serve two purposes. They give you a safe landing spot when re-entering from the bar, and they threaten your opponent's checkers as they try to come home. The 20-point and 21-point are particularly strong anchor positions early in the game.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Pile Everything on One Point
walkthrough.text: New players often stack five or six checkers on a single point for safety, but this is wasteful. Those extra checkers are doing nothing productive. You only need two on a point to close it. Spread your checkers across multiple points to control more of the board. The ideal position has two to three checkers on each of several consecutive points, forming a wall your opponent cannot pass.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Run vs. When to Build
walkthrough.text: Backgammon is a balance between racing your checkers home and building strong defensive positions. If you are ahead in the race, focus on getting home quickly and avoid unnecessary contact. If you are behind, slow down and look for chances to hit your opponent. A trailing player benefits from keeping the game messy, while a leading player benefits from a clean sprint to the finish.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Watch out for these patterns. First, moving your back checkers too aggressively without support, leaving blots everywhere. Second, ignoring the doubling cube and never using it, which means you miss chances to capitalize on strong positions. Third, bearing off slowly by moving checkers within the home board instead of removing them. Always bear off when you can rather than shuffling checkers around. Fix these three habits and you will see immediate improvement.

summary.title: Prioritize Making Points
bullets: 2+ checkers on a point = closed to opponent
Focus on home board: 5-point and 4-point are key
Strong home board blocks bar re-entry

summary.title: Learn the Opening Moves
bullets: Standard best plays exist for every opening roll
3-1 → make the 5-point, 6-1 → make the bar point
Learning a few openings gives a big edge

summary.title: Minimize Blots in Dangerous Zones
bullets: Within 6 points of enemy = direct hit range (single die)
7-12 points away = combo hit range (two dice)
Leave blots far from opponents when unavoidable

summary.title: Use the Bar Wisely When You Hit
bullets: Hitting isn't always correct — check if it leaves you exposed
Best hits: send opponent back while keeping your checkers safe

summary.title: Keep an Anchor in Your Opponent's Board
bullets: Anchor = 2+ checkers on a point in opponent's home board
Provides safe re-entry from the bar
Threatens opponent's checkers coming home — 20/21-point ideal

summary.title: Do Not Pile Everything on One Point
bullets: Only need 2 checkers to close a point
Extra checkers on one point are unproductive
Spread across multiple points → control more of the board

summary.title: Know When to Run vs. When to Build
bullets: Ahead in race → sprint home, avoid contact
Behind in race → slow down, seek hits to catch up
Balance racing and positioning based on game state

summary.title: Common Beginner Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Moving back checkers aggressively without support → blots
Ignoring the doubling cube → missed value
Shuffling checkers in home board instead of bearing off

walkthrough.title: The Doubling Cube — When to Double
walkthrough.text: The doubling cube is the most skill-intensive part of backgammon. You should offer a double when you estimate your winning chances are around 70 percent or higher. If you are too conservative, you miss value. If you are too aggressive, your opponent will accept and you are stuck at higher stakes with less edge than you thought. The classic rule of thumb is to double when you have a clear advantage but your opponent still has enough chances that they should accept.

walkthrough.title: The Doubling Cube — When to Accept
walkthrough.text: Your opponent offers a double. Should you take it? The math says you should accept if you have at least a 25 percent chance of winning. If you drop, you lose one point for certain. If you take and lose, you lose two. But if you take and win, you gain two. With 25 percent winning chances, taking and dropping have the same expected outcome. Anything above 25 percent, take the cube.

walkthrough.title: Building a Prime
walkthrough.text: A prime is six consecutive closed points, an impenetrable wall. No die roll can jump over six consecutive points. Building a prime while your opponent has checkers trapped behind it is one of the strongest positions in backgammon. Start by making adjacent points in your outer board and home board, then extend the wall. Even a partial prime of four or five consecutive points severely restricts your opponent's movement.

walkthrough.title: The Back Game
walkthrough.text: When you fall far behind in the race, a back game can be your path to victory. Hold two or more anchors deep in your opponent's home board and wait for them to leave blots as they bear off. The key is timing — you need enough checkers still in play that when you hit a blot, you can contain your opponent behind your own home board. A back game is risky but can produce dramatic comebacks when executed well.

walkthrough.title: Pip Count and Racing Decisions
walkthrough.text: The pip count is the total number of points all your checkers must travel to bear off. Tracking your pip count relative to your opponent's tells you who is ahead in the race. If you are ahead by 10 percent or more, consider breaking contact and running. If you are behind, keep the position complex and look for hitting chances. Many strong players count pips periodically throughout the game to guide their strategy.

walkthrough.title: Duplication and Diversification
walkthrough.text: When leaving blots, use duplication — arrange your vulnerable checkers so your opponent needs the same specific numbers to hit both. If your opponent needs fives to hit one blot and fives to hit another, they can only capitalize on one per turn. On offense, diversify — spread your threats so different numbers hit different targets. This maximizes your chances of hitting something useful regardless of what you roll.

walkthrough.title: Bearing Off Efficiently
walkthrough.text: During bearing off, avoid gaps in your home board. A gap on the three-point means every three you roll is wasted moving a checker down instead of removing one. Before you start bearing off, try to spread your checkers evenly across all six home board points. During bearing off, always remove a checker when possible rather than moving one within the home board. Each checker removed is permanent progress that cannot be taken back.

walkthrough.title: Gammon and Backgammon Awareness
walkthrough.text: Always be aware of gammon and backgammon threats, both yours and your opponent's. If you are in a commanding position, consider whether you can win a gammon by preventing your opponent from bearing off any checkers. Conversely, if you are losing badly, prioritize bearing off at least one checker to avoid being gammoned. In cube games, gammon awareness directly affects doubling decisions, since a gammon doubles the stakes again on top of the cube value.

summary.title: The Doubling Cube — When to Double
bullets: Double at ~70% win probability
Too conservative → miss value, too aggressive → bad stakes
Double when you have a clear edge but opponent should still take

summary.title: The Doubling Cube — When to Accept
bullets: Accept if win probability ≥ 25%
Drop = lose 1 point, take and lose = lose 2, take and win = gain 2
25% is the breakeven threshold

summary.title: Building a Prime
bullets: 6 consecutive closed points = impassable wall
Even 4-5 consecutive points severely restrict opponent
Build outward from home board through outer board

summary.title: The Back Game
bullets: Hold 2+ anchors deep in opponent's home board
Wait for blots during opponent's bearing off
Requires good timing — enough checkers to contain after hitting

summary.title: Pip Count and Racing Decisions
bullets: Pip count = total points all your checkers must travel
Ahead by 10%+ → break contact and run
Behind → keep position complex, seek hitting chances

summary.title: Duplication and Diversification
bullets: Duplication: make opponent need same numbers for multiple threats
Diversification: spread your threats across different numbers
Defense = duplicate, Offense = diversify

summary.title: Bearing Off Efficiently
bullets: Avoid gaps — rolled numbers with no checker waste the move
Spread evenly across home board before starting
Always remove a checker rather than moving within home board

summary.title: Gammon and Backgammon Awareness
bullets: Winning: push for gammon by preventing any opponent bear-off
Losing: bear off at least 1 checker to avoid gammon
Gammon threat affects doubling cube decisions

entries.definition: One of the 24 narrow triangles on the backgammon board. Points are numbered 1-24 from each player's perspective, with the 1-point in the player's home board and the 24-point in the opponent's home board.
The quadrant containing points 1 through 6. A player must move all 15 checkers into their home board before bearing off. Also called the inner board.
The quadrant containing points 7 through 12. Sits between the home board and the bar. Also called the outer table.
The raised ridge running down the center of the board, dividing it into two halves. Hit checkers are placed on the bar and must re-enter from the opponent's home board.
A unit of movement. Moving a checker from the 8-point to the 5-point costs 3 pips. The pip count is the total distance all of a player's checkers must travel to bear off completely.
A point with two or more of the same player's checkers. The opponent cannot land on a closed point. Consecutive closed points form a blockade.
Six consecutive closed points. No single die roll can exceed 6, so an opponent's checker behind a prime is completely trapped until the prime is broken.
A point in the opponent's home board occupied by two or more of your checkers. Provides a safe re-entry point from the bar and threatens the opponent's returning checkers.
A single checker sitting alone on a point. Blots are vulnerable to being hit by the opponent. Leaving blots in high-traffic areas is risky.
Landing on a point occupied by a single opponent checker (a blot). The hit checker is placed on the bar and must re-enter before its owner can make any other moves.

Moving a checker from the bar onto the opponent's home board. The player rolls their dice and places the checker on the point matching one die value (1-6), provided that point is open. If no point is open, the turn is forfeited.

Removing checkers from the board entirely. Requires all 15 checkers to be in the home board first. Roll a die matching a point number to remove a checker from that point. If no checker occupies the rolled point, move from a higher point or remove from the highest occupied point.
A die roll that matches a point with a checker on it during bearing off. For example, rolling a 4 when you have a checker on the 4-point lets you bear that checker off directly.

Die values that cannot be fully used during bearing off due to gaps in the home board. For example, rolling a 5 when your highest checker is on the 3-point. In this case, the checker on the 3-point is removed.

A large die marked 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 used to raise the stakes. A player may propose a double before rolling. The opponent either accepts (takes the cube) or forfeits at the current stake. Only the player holding the cube may propose the next double.
Accepting a double. The player takes the doubling cube and places it on their side showing the new stake value. They now own the cube and are the only one who may propose the next double.
Declining a double. The player forfeits the game immediately and loses the current stake value (before the proposed double). Also called passing or refusing the cube.
A win where the losing player has not borne off any checkers. Worth 2 times the current stake (doubled by the cube value if in play). A significant penalty for falling too far behind.
A win where the losing player has not borne off any checkers AND still has a checker on the bar or in the winner's home board. Worth 3 times the current stake. The most punishing outcome possible.
A series of games played to a target score (e.g., first to 7 points). Points from individual games (including gammon/backgammon multipliers and cube values) accumulate across the match.

In match play, when one player is exactly 1 point from winning, the doubling cube cannot be used for that one game. After the Crawford game, the cube may be used again. A standard rule in tournament play.
One of the 15 round playing pieces each player uses. Checkers are moved around the board according to dice rolls and ultimately borne off to win. Also called men, stones, or pieces.
A checker positioned to help make a new point on a future turn. Builders are often placed where they can reach multiple target points with common dice rolls.
Deliberately placing a single checker (blot) on a point you want to make, hoping to cover it with a second checker on a later turn. Risky but aggressive — the blot can be hit before it is covered.
A strategy focused on racing checkers home as fast as possible while avoiding contact with the opponent. Preferred when ahead in pip count.
